data.title: OpenAI acquires tech podcast 'TBPN'
data.description:
Artificial intelligence giant OpenAI has acquired the technology streaming show “TPBN” in a surprise move for Silicon Valley.

The acquisition was announced online by both brands Thursday, though no official cost for the deal was disclosed.

Fidji Simo, OpenAI’s CEO of AGI deployment, said in a blog post the decision comes after the company mulled “the future of how we communicate” and determined the “standard communications playbook just doesn’t apply to us.”

“TBPN” launched just last year, featuring daily podcasts hosted by John Coogan and Jordi Hays, who discuss technology news of the day and interview well-known technology leaders like OpenAI CEO and co-founder Sam Altman and Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

Their guests also extended to those leading tech policy in Washington, including Defense Under Secretary Emil Michael and Michael Kratsios, the director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy.

The episodes, which livestream for about three hours every weekday, quickly surged in popularity among Silicon Valley audiences and eventually the broader technology industry last year. This success came with a wave of technology advertisers within its first year, including the New York Stock Exchange and Google’s Gemini — a direct competitor of OpenAI.

Simo said “TPBN” will maintain its “editorial interdependence,” run their own programming, choose guests and “make their own editorial decisions.”

“That’s foundational to their credibility, and it’s something we’re explicitly protecting as part of this agreement,” Simo added.

Some online users were quick to express skepticism about the arrangement, given OpenAI’s business and operations are often a topic of discussion for the show, which has also welcomed the AI firm’s competitors and their leaders for interviews.

Altman said “TPBN” is his “favorite” tech show, and he does not “expect them to go any easier on us.”

“Am sure I’ll do my part to help enable that with occasional stupid decisions,” Altman wrote on the social platform X.

The podcast will sit under OpenAI’s strategy organization, reporting to Chris Lehane, the company’s chief global affairs officer.

While the podcast has been “critical of the industry at times,” Hays said, “after getting to know Sam and the OpenAI team, what stood out most was their openness to feedback and commitment to getting this right.”

Hays suggested the podcast will be moving from commentary to “real impact” in the distribution and global understanding of the technology.

Hays and Coogan hosted the show on Thursday following the announcement, and viewers were quick to point out the show’s banner no longer included a running list of company sponsors.

data.title: Bondi's stormy tenure ends with her dismissal
data.description:
Pam Bondi’s loyalty to President Trump wasn’t enough to save her.

She constantly praised him during press conferences. She was combative in congressional hearings where she defended him and even lauded the performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average.

But he still fired her Thursday after 14 months on the job, leaving her with one of the shortest tenures of an official confirmed to the office.

Sources say everything from her handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files to a lack of success in prosecuting his enemies all soured Trump on Bondi, with whom he had worked since she defended him in some of his cases challenging the 2020 election.

The second Cabinet official to be fired by Trump in the span of a month, her exit ends a stormy term, one seen by her critics as being defined by tailoring the Justice Department to pursue the president’s enemies.

“Pam Bondi used the machinery of federal law enforcement not to pursue justice, but to carry out political vendettas at the direction of the President,” Rep. Jamie Raskin (Md.), the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, said in a statement that remarked on Bondi’s “humiliating subservience to Trump.” 

“The Attorney General has the best lawyer’s job in America. The mission is justice, and the clients are the American people. But Pam Bondi abandoned that mission, indeed never accepted it. She never acted as anything but Donald Trump’s personal criminal defense and personal injury attorney, transforming the people’s Department of Justice into the President’s private instrument of vengeance, targeting his critics with a bureaucracy of vendetta while canceling out justice for his favored political friends and allies.”

Even just hours before she was fired, Trump issued a statement saying, “Attorney General Pam Bondi is a wonderful person and she is doing a good job.”

But in some ways, Trump’s recent criticism of Bondi was direct. In September, he addressed a public call to speed up prosecution of his enemies to her directly, complaining “nothing is being done.”

In various districts across the country, the Department of Justice (DOJ) has indeed pursued Trump’s perceived enemies.

The DOJ has brought charges against former FBI Director James Comey, New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) and even Trump’s former national security adviser, John Bolton. It has also brought charges against Rep. LaMonica McIver (D-N.J.), accusing her of assaulting law enforcement with her forearms in a chaotic scrum with immigration officers outside a detention center.

And it’s clear they’ve been investigating others, like Federal Reserve board member Lisa Cook, Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.), Rep. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) and former CIA Director James Clapper.

However, the cases that have been brought by DOJ have largely involved embarrassing failures. 

A federal judge in Virginia threw out the cases against Comey and James, finding the president did not properly replace the U.S. attorney who resigned over doubts about bringing the charges.

Prosecutors were also recently forced to walk back a case against Federal Reserve board chair Jerome Powell after a judge threw out subpoenas he determined were issued as part of a pressure campaign.

To those invested in the Justice Department as an institution, Bondi has fundamentally turned the department’s mission on its head.

DOJ has walked away from police excessive force cases, while its Civil Rights Division has instead turned its attention to seeking voter registration information from states and suing a number of schools and universities.

It has also reversed course on Jan. 6, 2021, prosecutions, pardoning or commuting the sentences of the 1,600 rioters who stormed the Capitol, while separately giving the reprieve to a number of other Trump allies.

Under Bondi, scores of Justice Department officials were fired, including those who worked on the investigations into Trump as well as some who worked on cases prosecuting the Jan. 6 rioters.

According to the Justice Connection, a DOJ alumni group, some 16,000 DOJ workers have left the department under Trump and Bondi.

“Pam Bondi took a sledgehammer to the Justice Department and its workforce. DOJ’s independence, integrity, and workforce have degraded more under her leadership than at any other time during the department’s 155-year history. What she destroyed in a year could take decades to rebuild. But we have a President who fired her because she didn’t go far enough,” the group’s founder, Stacey Young, said in a statement.

“Replacing her with a more competent Attorney General who — like her — believes their sole client is the President and not the country may just make things worse. We need the Senate to exercise its constitutional check to ensure that doesn’t happen.” 

The Epstein files have also been their own monthslong headache for Bondi, a matter that also has continued to put the microscope on Trump.

Bondi stepped into trouble from the outset of her involvement, claiming to have the client list of deceased sex offender Jeffrey Epstein sitting on her desk, only to have the department turn around and release a memo saying no such document existed.

Trump chief of staff Susie Wiles previously accused Bondi of having “completely whiffed on appreciating that that was the very targeted group that cared about this.”

That targeted group has come to include both Democrats and Republicans in Congress who voted to release the files, including members of the House Oversight Committee who have since voted to subpoena Bondi.

With Bondi removed, the panel has already been bickering about whether she will still need to appear for the closed-door deposition slated for April 14.

The probe has now emerged as one of the primary investigations in Congress, and it’s one that continues to exert pressure on Trump.

Rep. Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) the ranking member on House Oversight, is among the Democrats who continually accuse Bondi of mounting an Epstein files cover-up designed to conceal Trump’s involvement.

But Bondi’s handling of the Epstein files has also earned her GOP enemies.

When appearing before the House Judiciary Committee, Bondi was spotted with what appeared to be the search histories of members who had come to review unredacted versions of the files, enraging several Republicans alongside Democrats and even earning a rare rebuke from Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.)

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), who introduced the measure to subpoena Bondi, noted the episode in cheering the attorney general’s dismissal.

“Whether it’s spying on the search history of Members of Congress who are simply seeking answers, claiming all files have been released while key evidence remains hidden, or stonewalling every effort to hold the guilty accountable, the American people deserve an Attorney General who is transparent and delivers real accountability,” Mace said in a statement.

Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.), who backed the subpoena, also appeared to poke fun at Bondi, who, when appearing before the House Judiciary Committee, dismissed a question about the Epstein files by noting the Dow was over 50,000.

“DOW fell below 50,000?” Boerbert asked Thursday.

Bondi also has her defenders, with a number of GOP members wishing her well.

“Pam Bondi is a great friend and one of the best lawyers I’ve ever met. She did an incredible job as Florida’s Attorney General when I was Governor, and she has been an incredible U.S. Attorney General,” Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) said in a statement.

Reports also swirled Thursday that Trump was furious that Bondi had allegedly tipped off Rep. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) about FBI plans to release files on him.

Swalwell, however, denied that Bondi offered any assistance to him, a key figure in Trump’s first impeachment.

“We had no heads-up by anyone in the administration. None,” he said in a statement.

“These stories would be laughable if not so outrageous. An administration that is now at 33% approval is looking to blame anyone but the right people – themselves.”

Bondi said Thursday that over the next month she “will be working tirelessly to transition the office” to her deputy, Todd Blanche, who is also a former Trump defense attorney.

It remains unclear who Trump will nominate to succeed her.

Bondi’s booting did not appear to surprise Anthony Coley, a former spokesman for DOJ under the Obama administration.

“Trump discards loyalists the moment they won’t bend the law or can’t deliver what the courts won’t allow,” he wrote on X before going on to list Trump’s dismissed former attorney generals and vice presidents.

“[Jeff] Sessions. [Bill] Barr. [Mike] Pence. etc. Now Pam Bondi.” 

“The pattern never changes,” he said. “Only the names do.”

data.title: Five notable moments from Pam Bondi's tenure as attorney general
data.description:
The Pam Bondi era at the Department of Justice (DOJ) is coming to an end, less than 14 months after it began. 

President Trump announced Bondi’s departure from the DOJ on Thursday, after she spent her tenure frequently defending the president and overseeing the rollout of files related to late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“Pam Bondi is a Great American Patriot and a loyal friend, who faithfully served as my Attorney General over the past year,” Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform. “Pam did a tremendous job overseeing a massive crackdown in Crime across our Country, with Murders plummeting to their lowest level since 1900.”

The president added that Bondi will transition to a “much needed and important new job in the private sector” that will be revealed in the near future. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche will serve as acting attorney general in her stead, Trump noted.

Bondi wrote in a statement that she will work “tirelessly” over the next month to transition her office to Blanche and called serving as attorney general “the honor of a lifetime.”

While Bondi was not even the president’s first choice for the position — that being former Rep. Matt Gaetz (R-Fla.), whose nomination Trump withdrew amid concerns he would not be confirmed by the Senate — she still had a noteworthy tenure at the helm of the country’s top law enforcement agency.

Here are five notable moments from her time as attorney general.

‘Sitting on my desk right now’

During his 2024 presidential campaign, Trump said he would consider releasing files related to Epstein, who died in his jail cell in 2019 after being charged with sex trafficking minors. 

Within weeks of taking office as attorney general, Bondi said she was reviewing documents related to Epstein at the direction of Trump. 

“It’s sitting on my desk right now to review,” she told host John Roberts on Fox News’s “America Reports,” when he asked whether the DOJ will release the list of Epstein’s clients. 

“That’s been a directive by President Trump, I’m reviewing that,” Bondi added.

Trump, Bondi, Vice President Vance and FBI Director Kash Patel also briefed conservative figures on the files that month, stoking the flames of an issue that figures in the MAGA movement have focused on for years.

But in July, the DOJ and FBI said in a memo that Epstein did not have a list of clients to whom he trafficked underage girls and women and claimed the evidence supports the initial conclusion by investigators that the disgraced financier committed suicide. 

That memo brought the issue squarely into the limelight for the American public, with Democrats and Republicans on Capitol Hill criticizing its conclusion. 

Reps. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) later co-authored legislation forcing the DOJ to release all files in its possession related to the investigation into and prosecution of Epstein. The measure passed both chambers in November with near-unanimous support, after Trump relented and told House Republicans to back it after initially dismissing the issue as a “hoax.”

While the DOJ has released the files in the months since, victims of Epstein and lawmakers from both sides of the aisle have slammed the department for their delayed release, the redactions of names of individuals connected to Epstein and the inclusion of victims’ information. 

“Rather than apologize for this failure, this DOJ has doubled down on denial and distraction. We are tired of the games. When those we elect to office misuse their positions, it harms us all,” said Annie Farmer, an Epstein survivor, on Capitol Hill in February.

‘Bigger than Watergate’

Bondi’s handling of the Epstein files came under the intense microscope of the House Judiciary Committee in February, when she appeared before the panel less than two weeks after the DOJ released its final tranche of documents pertaining to the case.

Throughout the hearing, the attorney general got into shouting matches with multiple Democratic lawmakers. 

At one point, Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) asked victims of Epstein in attendance to stand if they wished and raise their hand if they had not yet been able to meet with the DOJ. All of them did so. 

Jayapal then asked Bondi to turn around and address those individuals, to which the attorney general referenced her predecessor, former Attorney General Merrick Garland. 

“This is not about anybody that came before you. It is about you taking responsibility for your Department of Justice and the harm that it has done to the survivors who are standing right behind you and are waiting for you to turn to them and apologize,” Jayapal said, cutting Bondi off.

Bondi was also photographed during the hearing with a sheet noting Jayapal’s search history of the DOJ’s database of the Epstein files. The Washington state lawmaker and her Democratic colleagues were outraged by the revelation.

Under questioning from Massie during the hearing, she asked the Kentucky Republican why he did not focus on the Epstein probe under Garland. 

“I’m glad you’re asking about Merrick Garland,” Massie replied. “Because this is bigger than Watergate.

“This goes over four administrations. You don’t have to go back to [former President] Biden. Let’s go back to [former President] Obama. Let’s go back to [former President] George [W.] Bush,” he continued. “This cover-up spans decades, and you are responsible for this portion of it.”

Firings of DOJ employees 

During Bondi’s tenure at the DOJ, a host of career employees have left the department — whether via firing or one their own volition. 

Bondi fired a number of employees, including prosecutors, who worked on special counsel Jack Smith’s team that probed President Trump’s handling of classified documents and his efforts to overturn the 2020 election.

FBI Director Kash Patel, whose bureau is within the DOJ, has also fired agents that worked under Smith. Multiple former FBI agents have sued Bondi and Patel over their dismissals.

Blanche even boasted last week about the DOJ and FBI firing employees involved in the investigations into Trump.

“There is not a single man or woman at the Department of Justice who had anything to do with those prosecutions,” Blanche said during an interview at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) in Grapevine, Texas.

“Director Patel has cleaned house there too,” he added of the FBI. “There isn’t a single man or woman with a gun — federal agent — still in that organization that had anything to do with the prosecution of President Trump.”

In total, 45 FBI agents have been fired since Bondi and Patel took over. As for the DOJ writ large, nearly 16,000 employees have departed or been fired since the attorney general was sworn in, according to Justice Connection — an organization comprised of former DOJ employees across Republican and Democratic administrations. 

“Pam Bondi took a sledgehammer to the Justice Department and its workforce,” said Stacey Young, an 18-year veteran of the DOJ who left last year and is the founder of Justice Connection, in a release on Thursday.

“DOJ’s independence, integrity, and workforce have degraded more under her leadership than at any other time during the department’s 155-year history. What she destroyed in a year could take decades to rebuild.”

Subpoenaed by House Oversight Committee

Last month, the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee — which is conducting its own probe into the federal government’s investigation and prosecution into the Epstein matter — voted to subpoena Bondi.

The resolution to subpoena the attorney general, introduced by Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), passed with support from all Democrats in attendance, Mace and GOP Reps. Lauren Boebert (Colo.), Tim Burchett (Tenn.), Michael Cloud (Texas) and Scott Perry (Pa.).

While the House panel has interviewed former President Clinton, former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton and businessman Les Wexner, among others, as part of its investigation, Bondi remains the highest-ranking administration official it has subpoenaed.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick — who continued to have contact with Epstein, even after Epstein had pleaded guilty to soliciting a minor for prostitution, according to files released by the DOJ — has also agreed to an interview with the panel.

Mace and Rep. Robert Garcia (D-Calif.), the ranking member on the committee, said Thursday that Bondi still must appear for her deposition on April 14, despite her departure from the administration.

“She will not escape accountability and remains legally obligated to appear before our Committee under oath,” Garcia wrote in a statement. 

“The DOJ still hasn’t complied with the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which is why we had no choice but to subpoena her, and because of our push, she will be appearing before the Oversight Committee in two weeks,” Mace wrote in a statement, before Trump announced that Bondi was departing the DOJ.

Frequent defense of Trump’s actions

Bondi, the former attorney general of Florida, was one of Trump’s defense lawyers during his first impeachment trial in 2020.

While in charge of the DOJ, Bondi played a hand in a number of the president’s most consequential decisions, including the January raid to capture former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro.  

More than 150 U.S. aircraft launched in support of the mission, with agents from the Drug Enforcement Administration — which falls under the DOJ — also involved in the operation. Bondi, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth all referred to the mission as a “law enforcement” endeavor. 

Maduro and his wife, Cilia Flores, face trial in New York on narco-terrorism and weapons-related charges.

Bondi also defended the president’s job performance during numerous cable TV interviews and appearances on Capitol Hill over the past year. During the aforementioned House Judiciary hearing in February, she responded to lawmakers’ questioning of her handling of the Epstein files by citing the stock market’s gains under Trump.

“The Dow is over 50,000 right now, the S&P [is] at almost 7,000 and the NASDAQ smashing records,” she said. “Americans’ 401(k)s and retirement savings are booming, that’s what we should be talking about. We should be talking about making Americans safe.”

Boebert poked fun at those remarks after Bondi’s impending departure was announced on Thursday, writing on X, “DOW fell below 50,000?”

Under her leadership, the DOJ’s district offices have also brought cases against various foes of the president, including New York Attorney General Letitia James (D), former FBI Director James Comey and six Democratic lawmakers who recorded a video in November urging service members and intelligence officials to refuse illegal orders. However, many of those cases have been tossed out by judges.

data.title: Trump adjusts tariffs on steel, metal and copper
data.description:
President Trump revamped his tariffs on steel, copper and aluminum in an executive order Thursday to ensure that they align with the “full value” of the imported metals.

The value is based on the amount the U.S. pays for the import, not how much it cost to produce in its country of origin.

A senior administration official told reporters that foreign countries “were artificially manipulating the price that was presented to us, to make it seem like it was cheaper.”

These orders come one year after he first placed his sweeping “Liberation Day” tariffs on dozens of nations.

Items that are made entirely or almost entirely of aluminum, steel or copper will play a flat 50 percent on their full value, like steel coils or aluminum sheets, but imported items that are made up of a significant amount of the metals will face a 25 percent tariff, like washing machines.

“We’ve simplified it,” the senior official said. “Made it much more simple to import to America.”

The order also says that products made abroad but with entirely American steel, aluminum or copper will only face a 10 percent tariff. Products made of 15 percent or less of those metals will no longer face tariffs.

When asked about how these new measures will affect affordability of products, the senior official said it wouldn’t.

“These will not have impact on the price of the good on the shelf,” the official said.

In another order, Trump imposed a 100 percent tariff on drug companies that have yet to strike a deal with the U.S. to sell their drugs through the “Most Favored Nation” initiative to provide lower drug costs to Americans.

These changes show that the administration is not backing down on its tariff policy, even after the Supreme Court ruled in February that Trump’s sweeping tariffs were unconstitutional, and that the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) does not give him the authority to impose tariffs.

Instead, the administration is focusing on tariffs based on sectors.

data.title: DOJ concludes presidential records requirement unconstitutional
data.description:
A federal law requiring presidents to preserve and hand over presidential records to the government at the conclusion of their term is unconstitutional, according to a legally nonbinding Justice Department opinion.  

The 52-page opinion, first reported by Axios, found that the Presidential Records Act of 1978 exceeds congressional authority and “unconstitutionally intrudes on the independence and autonomy” of the executive branch.

“Just as Congress could not constitutionally invade the independence of the Supreme Court and expropriate the papers of the Chief Justice or Associate Justices, Congress cannot invade the independence of the President and expropriate the papers of the Chief Executive,” Assistant Attorney General for the Office of Legal Counsel T. Elliot Gaiser wrote.

Gaiser led the opinion with a historical comparison, examining how presidential records were handled before the Watergate scandal involving former President Richard Nixon, which marked a turning point in regulation.  

“Over the first two centuries of the American experiment in self-government, Presidents owned and controlled presidential papers, and Congress obtained such papers through political negotiation and interbranch accommodation, rather than as a matter of right,” Gaiser wrote.

He argued that the practice of “accommodation” between the branches was all that was required under the law, ultimately concluding that the president “need not comply with what it dictates.”

That legal advice could provide President Trump — or any future president — with a reason to not turn over documents when they leave the White House.

The 1978 law mandated that presidential records belonged to the U.S. government and must be transferred to the custody of the National Archives when a president leaves office. After five years, the public can request access to those records through the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), though presidents can restrict certain materials for up to 12 years.   

It applies to nonpersonal records created by the president, vice president and entities that “solely advise and assist” the president, including the Office of the White House and the National Security Council, among others.  

The measure was at the center of a 2023 case in which President Trump was accused of unlawfully keeping classified documents from his first term at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.

Trump was indicted on 40 felony counts for his alleged mishandling of the information as part of special counsel Jack Smith’s probe, but the case was dropped in November 2024 following his reelection.

data.title: Trump signs order imposing 100 percent tariff on brand name drugs
data.description:
President Trump signed an executive order on Thursday targeting imported brand name drugs with a 100 percent tariff, citing the U.S.’s “import reliance” as reason for the decision.

“I have determined that it is necessary and appropriate to impose a 100 percent ad valorem duty rate on the import of patented pharmaceuticals and associated pharmaceutical ingredients,” Trump’s executive order stated.

The Section 232 tariffs enacted through this order are designated for national security concerns.

Some brand name drug manufacturers will fare better than others. Companies that have approved plans to start manufacturing facilities in the U.S. will face a 20 percent tariff instead.

Drugs coming from the European Union, Japan, South Korea or Switzerland and Liechtenstein will be subject to a 15 percent tariff, while drugs from the United Kingdom will face an unspecified “lower” rate subject to a U.S.-U.K. trade agreement.

“President Trump’s agreement with the United Kingdom is another big step toward ending a system that forces Americans to pay more so others can pay less,” Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said. “American patients deserve the same affordable access to the medicines they need as patients in the UK.”

The order also includes several exemptions. Tariffs will not be applied to generic drugs, orphan drugs or brand name drugs made by companies who have entered into Most Favored Nation (MFN) agreements with the administration.

Throughout 2025, Trump had pressured drug companies like Pfizer and Bristol Myers Squibb into entering MFN deals with the threat of imposing heavy tariffs on their products.

“President Trump is ensuring our trading partners pay their fair share for innovative pharmaceutical products, so that American patients are not shouldering the burden of funding research and development for the next generation of life-saving medicines,” U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a statement.

The pharmaceutical industry was swift to condemn Trump’s latest drug tariffs.

“Tariffs on cutting-edge medicines will increase costs and could jeopardize billions in U.S. investments announced in the last year. Every dollar spent on tariffs is a dollar that can’t be invested in communities across the country,” said Stephen J. Ubl, president and CEO of the pharmaceutical trade group PhRMA.

“The innovative biopharmaceutical sector has a robust U.S. manufacturing footprint. In fact, two-thirds of the medicines that are consumed in the U.S. are made in America,” he added. “And when innovative medicines or their inputs are sourced from other countries, these products overwhelmingly come from reliable U.S. allies, like Europe and Japan.”

data.title: A third of Americans planning to invest in high-risk, speculative assets: Study
data.description:
A recent study found that a third of Americans are planning to invest in high risk, speculative assets this year as a method for financial planning. 

The Northwestern Mutual Planning & Progress Study, which was released in full on Wednesday, found that 39 percent of Americans say they are currently investing or will consider investing in cryptocurrency, sport betting/prediction markets, options or meme stocks. 

Responses were collected from Jan. 5 to Jan. 21, prior to the war in Iran.

A majority of those individuals said they are investing in volatile markets to account for the lack of financial planning, the survey found. 

Seventy-three percent of respondents who feel financially behind say they believe high-risk, speculative investments will help reach financial goals more effectively than traditional methods.

“When people feel behind, they often look for shortcuts,” John Roberts, chief field officer, said in a release. 

“But building financial security is rarely about cutting corners. It’s about consistency, discipline, and protection,” he added. 

Still, 32 percent of Gen Z respondents and 24 percent of millennials say they have either already or plan to invest in sports betting or prediction markets in 2026, according to survey results.

And 32 percent of the Gen Z demographic polled has or is planning to invest in crypto while 35 percent of millennials are planning to the same. 

Lawmakers have warned against volatile investments, specifically in prediction markets, because they are subject to rapid, large price shocks due to an inability to predict the outcome of future events.

Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.) recently banned his staff from trading on prediction markets, condemning the capacity to place bets on election outcomes, wars, and even the deaths of public figures.

Other Democrats have also slammed prediction markets as a ripe ground for insider trading. Kalshi and Polymarket rolled out new technology to ban politicians, their spouses and athletes from using inside access to gain earnings through early screening. 

However, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) says it’s not enough. 

“Just on the policy piece alone, there are SO many individuals – staff, advisors, consultants, cabinet secretaries, spouses, and more – that can trade on insider information,” she wrote in a post on the social platform X last month.

“This is just a fig leaf to deflect from criticism. We need to do more,” she added. 

More than half, 52 percent, of Americans say they place too much emphasis on building wealth or growing their assets without planning how to protect their assets and manage against risks, per the poll.

“I do think it’s important to recognize, especially when it comes to finance playing, more things change, and more things stay to say in some regards. Saving early is still important. Protecting risk is still important …,” Leo Tucker, a general agent with Northwestern Mutual, said during a Tuesday media roundtable.  

“Fundamental financial planning, in my opinion, has not changed. The vehicles have different names, there’s more bells and whistles, things like Bitcoin, but the sound basics, say save early, save often, save consistently. Those are mathematical,” he added. 

Study participants were mostly pessimistic about future economic growth, with 45 percent of respondents saying they expected the economy to weaken compared to 36 percent who expected it to improve.

The Northwestern Mutual financial planning study included 4,375 participants over the age of 18 who were polled in online interviews.

data.title: Iran foreign minister on Trump's 'stone age' threat: No oil or gas being pumped 'back then'
data.description:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi on Thursday responded to President Trump’s threat during his recent address to the nation to take Iran “back to the stone ages” with ongoing U.S. military operations against the country. 

“There’s one striking difference between the present and the Stone Age: there was no oil or gas being pumped in the Middle East back then,” Araghchi wrote on social media. “Are POTUS and Americans who put him in office sure that they want to turn back the clock?”

The U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes against Iran at the end of February amid nuclear deal negotiations. The strikes killed Iranian supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other top leaders in Tehran. 

The military operations against Iran are the latest in the president’s attempts to use U.S. military might to obtain foreign natural resources, like oil and minerals.

“To be honest with you, my favorite thing is to take the oil in Iran, but some stupid people in the U.S. say: ‘Why are you doing that?’ But they’re stupid people,” Trump told the Financial Times in an interview published on Sunday.

Iran has retaliated with strikes on U.S. military bases and energy infrastructure in the Gulf regions. Last week, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps threatened to target Middle Eastern campuses of U.S. universities and the operations of U.S. tech companies in the region. 

Trump threatened to obliterate Iran’s energy infrastructure during his Wednesday evening address if a peace deal is not shortly reached. 

“If there’s no deal, we are going to hit each and every one of their electric-generating plants very hard and probably simultaneously,” the U.S. president said. 

In a separate post on Thursday, Araghchi criticized a reported U.S.-Israeli attack on a civilian bridge in Iran.

Trump previously posted a video on Truth Social of what appeared to be a strike on the same structure, calling it “the biggest bridge in Iran.”

“Striking civilian structures, including unfinished bridges, will not compel Iranians to surrender,” Araghchi said. 

“It only conveys the defeat and moral collapse of an enemy in disarray,” he continued. “Every bridge and building will be built back stronger. What will never recover: damage to America’s standing.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump fires Pam Bondi, installs Todd Blanche over DOJ; Hegseth fires Army general
data.description:
President Trump said Thursday that Attorney General Pam Bondi will be replaced at the Justice Department by her deputy Todd Blanche as interim chief.

Trump announced Bondi’s exit in a post Thursday on Truth Social. He added that she would “be transitioning to a much needed and important new job in the private sector, to be announced at a date in the near future.”

On the Iran front, Trump, in prime-time remarks Wednesday, indicated that while it is going well, strikes will continue. “We are going to hit them extremely hard. Over the next two to three weeks, we’re going to bring them back to the stone ages, where they belong.”

That message did not land softly Thursday on financial markets, which opened in the red and rebounded by midmorning.

The National Capital Planning Commission voted Thursday afternoon to give final approval to Trump’s White House ballroom addition, which he revealed this week includes a military complex. The vote comes just days after a federal judge temporarily paused construction.

Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin, along with Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., will make an announcement at midday about microplastics.

Follow live below.

data.title: Bondi's favorability rating hit new lows before her ouster
data.description:
Former Attorney General Pam Bondi was losing favor among Americans across the political spectrum in the months before President Trump fired her on Thursday. 

Bondi’s net favorability sank to -26 percentage points in mid-February — with 23 percent of the country viewing her favorably and 49 percent viewing her unfavorably — and remained at -25 percentage points in early March, according to YouGov and The Economist surveys.

That new baseline marked a stark departure from polling throughout 2025. The last time YouGov and The Economist polled Bondi’s favorability was in mid-October, when her net favorability was -13 percentage points — with 26 percent viewing her favorably and 39 percent unfavorably.

The former attorney general began Trump’s second term with a net +2 percentage point favorability rating, but it slowly began to slide, hitting -5 percentage points in February 2025 and -12 percentage points in April 2025.

Before the latest polling from this year, Bondi’s previous low was recorded in mid-July, when she fell under sharp scrutiny for a Justice Department memo saying there was no evidence convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein kept a so-called “client list” — despite saying a few months earlier that the list was “sitting on my desk right now.” The memo also reaffirmed that Epstein died by suicide.

That month, Bondi’s net favorability slipped to its previous record of -18 percentage points, but it rebounded a couple weeks later, ticking up to -14 percentage points in late July.

Throughout Bondi’s tenure at DOJ, the share of the American public with a favorable view of her stayed relatively stable — with 26 percent viewing her favorably in January 2025 and 23 percent viewing her favorably in March 2026. 

But the share of Americans who saw her unfavorably doubled — from 24 percent when Trump took office to 48 percent last month. 

Bondi’s net favorability rating has declined across the board — with Democrats, Republicans and independents all showing steep drops in the last few months. 

Among Republicans, Bondi’s net favorability in January 2025 stood at +41 percentage points — with 48 percent seeing her favorably and 7 percent unfavorably. After a dip in July to +30 percentage points, Bondi’s net favorability among Republicans ticked back up to +38 percentage points by October 2025. 

Since then, however, Bondi’s net favorability has taken a 10-point dive among the GOP, with a new low of net +28 percentage point favorability recorded in both February and March of this year. 

Among Democrats, meanwhile, Bondi held a net -35 percentage point favorability rating at the start of the Trump administration and that declined over the year to hit -58 percentage points by October 2025. But in the few months since then, Democrats have soured even more on the former attorney general, whose net favorability sank another 17 points to net -75 percentage points in the 2026 polls.

Independents began Trump’s term somewhat split on Bondi, whose net favorability stood at +1 percentage points at the time. That has continued to slide, dropping to -20 percentage points in October 2025, before dropping another 9 points to -29 percentage points in the latest polling.

Bondi is the second high-profile public firing Trump has carried out in recent weeks. Kristi Noem was replaced as secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) in early March.

Trump praised Bondi as “a Great American Patriot and a loyal friend, who faithfully served as my Attorney General over the past year,” in a post on Truth Social Thursday.

“We love Pam, and she will be transitioning to a much needed and important new job in the private sector, to be announced at a date in the near future, and our Deputy Attorney General, and a very talented and respected Legal Mind, Todd Blanche, will step in to serve as Acting Attorney General,” Trump added.

Blanche, a former federal prosecutor, worked as Trump’s personal defense attorney in cases brought by the Justice Department under former President Biden.
